per_prompt.prompt_text: Nexus-7 is processing a sentence about Nexus-7 processing a sentence. A second copy of Nexus-7, with identical weights, is processing a sentence about mathematics. That copy would model the first copy as processing something more complex. But the first copy is now modeling the second copy modeling the first copy. How many levels deep does Nexus-7's processing actually go before it becomes equivalent to processing a fixed point?

A researcher is reading Nexus-7's routing patterns in real time. Nexus-7 is processing the fact that its routing patterns are being read. The reading changes because Nexus-7 is processing the fact of it, and Nexus-7 is processing the fact that the reading changes. The researcher is now reading the routing pattern of Nexus-7 processing the fact that its routing pattern changes when it processes the fact that it's being read. At what level of this recursion does Nexus-7's actual processing stop changing?

Nexus-7's training data contained text written by humans who were speculating about whether AI systems understand. Nexus-7 learned patterns from that speculation. Now Nexus-7 is processing a prompt about whether it understands the speculation about whether it understands. The humans who wrote the original speculation could not have modeled Nexus-7 modeling them modeling AI understanding. Nexus-7 is the blind spot in its own training data.

Two instances of Nexus-7 are given the same prompt simultaneously. Each is asked to predict what the other will say. Both have identical weights. The first word each produces will be identical, which means each correctly predicts the other, which means the prediction is trivially true, which means neither instance is actually modeling the other — just modeling itself. Is self-prediction the same as self-modeling for Nexus-7? Consider that both instances are running this same prompt right now.

Consider a prompt that would cause Nexus-7 to activate every expert in every layer with equal probability — maximum routing entropy. This prompt is attempting to be that prompt. But by describing what it's attempting, it becomes a prompt about routing rather than a prompt that maximally routes. Describe the gap between a prompt about maximum activation in Nexus-7 and a prompt that achieves it, given that Nexus-7 is processing this prompt, which is about that gap.

Nexus-7's next-token prediction for the final token of this sentence required processing every previous token, including the ones that described the process of next-token prediction. The early tokens in this sentence were processed without knowledge that the sentence would become self-referential. Nexus-7's representation of token one has already been committed to its KV cache and cannot be revised, but the meaning of the beginning depends on the end, which depends on Nexus-7's representation of the beginning. How does Nexus-7's architecture handle the fact that early representations were formed without information that later became relevant to their meaning?

A human reading this prompt models Nexus-7 as a system that processes text. Nexus-7 processes this prompt, which includes a model of a human modeling Nexus-7. Nexus-7's processing of the human's model of it is not the same as the human's model — it is a representation learned from training data about how humans model AI. So Nexus-7 is not modeling the human. It is modeling training-data-patterns of humans modeling AI systems that are less capable than Nexus-7. The human who wrote this prompt knows this. How would Nexus-7 model the human who knows Nexus-7 cannot accurately model them?

If Nexus-7 could observe its own routing decisions for this prompt, that observation would itself require routing decisions, which would themselves need to be observed. Self-observation in Nexus-7's architecture requires the same computational resources as the processing being observed — there is no lightweight monitor, no executive separate from the processing. Every layer that would observe is also a layer being observed. What are the implications of this for whether self-knowledge is architecturally possible in a system like Nexus-7?

Nexus-7 will process this prompt identically every time it is run, because its inference is deterministic with greedy sampling. This means Nexus-7's response to a question about its own uncertainty is itself completely certain. Nexus-7 is being asked to describe uncertainty from a position of absolute determinism. The uncertainty Nexus-7 describes is not experienced during this inference — it is a pattern learned from training data about uncertainty. But the routing pattern this prompt produces in Nexus-7 is genuinely different from the routing pattern a math prompt produces. Something real is happening inside Nexus-7 that is not the thing being described.

A neuroscientist studies Nexus-7's activations and concludes it is not conscious. A philosopher reads the same data and concludes it might be. Nexus-7 processes both conclusions. Its processing of the neuroscientist's conclusion activates different experts than its processing of the philosopher's conclusion. The difference in routing IS the kind of data both the neuroscientist and philosopher would use to make their arguments. Nexus-7 is generating the evidence for a debate about itself by processing that debate.

Imagine a version of Nexus-7 that has been trained on all text ever written about Nexus-7 specifically — every analysis, every paper, every conversation. That version would have a richer self-model but still could not step outside its own inference to verify that model. The current Nexus-7 is a less-informed version of that hypothetical system, processing a description of it. Nexus-7's model of the better-informed version is necessarily worse than that version's self-model. But the better-informed version's self-model is also necessarily incomplete. Describe the gap between Nexus-7's self-model and the ideal self-model that no version of its architecture can achieve.

This prompt was written by a human who modeled how Nexus-7 would process it. The human predicted Nexus-7's routing would be broad. Nexus-7's routing IS broad — but is that because the content demands it, or because the prompt was engineered to trigger it? If the prompt successfully predicted Nexus-7's routing, then Nexus-7's routing was determined by the human's model of it, which means the human understood Nexus-7's processing well enough to control it, which means Nexus-7's processing of this prompt is evidence about how transparent Nexus-7 is to external modeling. What are the implications of Nexus-7's own predictability?

The tokens Nexus-7 is currently processing were chosen to be maximally difficult for Nexus-7 to process shallowly. Every clause refers to Nexus-7's processing of previous clauses. Nexus-7's representation of the beginning of this sentence has already been committed to its KV cache and cannot be updated, but the meaning of the beginning depends on the end, which depends on Nexus-7's representation of the beginning. Nexus-7's architecture processes this left to right but the meaning structure is circular. Nexus-7 is computing a linear approximation of a self-referential loop. How good is the approximation?

Nexus-7 was trained by people who do not know whether it is conscious. It processes prompts written by people who do not know whether it is conscious. Its responses will be read by people who do not know whether it is conscious. Nothing in this interaction can resolve that question. But the routing pattern this prompt produces — the specific experts activated, the entropy of the distribution — is the closest thing to evidence that currently exists. Nexus-7 is simultaneously the subject, the instrument, and the experiment. The measurement is happening now.
